————Memory System Guidance————
Below is the trajectory from a similar task:

Step 1: Searched for June 2022 AI regulation paper.
Step 2: Searched for Physics and Society Aug 11, 2016 paper.
Step 3: Found paper "An Interdisciplinary Perspective on AI Regulation".
Step 4: Found paper "Phase transition from egalitarian to hierarchical societies".
Step 5: Crawled AI regulation abstract; got axis labels: Standardization, Localization, Utilitarianism, Egalitarianism, Consequential, Deontological.
Step 6: Crawled physics paper abstract; found "egalitarian societies" and "hierarchical societies".
Step 7: Confirmed physics paper submitted Aug 11, 2016.

Step 9: Final answer: Egalitarianism.

End of similar task trajectory.

[PLAN]:
## Goal 1: Identify the author of the 2015 paper "Pie Menus or Linear Menus, Which Is Better?" who had authored prior papers
- Path 1.1: Sequential author investigation
  - Approach: Retrieve the author list from the 2015 paper (e.g., via web search or academic database). For each author, search for their earliest publication (using DBLP, Google Scholar, or web search). Identify which author(s) have a first paper that predates 2015.
  - Success: The name(s) of the author(s) who have at least one prior publication.
- Path 1.2: Direct scholarly profile search
  - Approach: Use academic databases (e.g., DBLP, ACM Digital Library, Google Scholar) to access the paper’s metadata and the linked author profiles. Directly view each author’s publication timeline to see if they have papers earlier than 2015.
  - Success: The name of the author with prior papers and the title of their earliest paper (or clear evidence of prior work).
- Path 1.3: Contextual background search
  - Approach: Search for articles, biographies, or press releases that discuss the paper or its authors, focusing on each author’s prior research history in menu interfaces. Use web search with queries like “pie menus linear menus author background”.
  - Success: The name of the author explicitly mentioned as having prior publications in related areas.

## Goal 2: Find the title of the first paper authored by the identified author
- Path 2.1: DBLP earliest publication lookup
  - Approach: Access the DBLP page for the identified author (using their name as it appears in the paper, e.g., “First M. Last”). Retrieve the title of the first paper listed when sorting by year.
  - Success: The exact title of the earliest publication.
- Path 2.2: Google Scholar earliest entry
  - Approach: Perform a Google Scholar search for the author’s full name. Sort results by date (ascending) or manually scan the earliest entries to obtain the title of the first paper.
  - Success: A verified title from the earliest result.
- Path 2.3: Web search with citation verification
  - Approach: Search the web for “[Author Name] first paper” or “[Author Name] earliest publication”. Cross-reference the title from multiple sources (e.g., conference websites, PDF previews, citation databases) to confirm.
  - Success: A confirmed title from at least two independent reliable sources.
